channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Massachusetts Teacher Charged With Raping Two Students
item.description: The students say the former teacher at Miss Hall’s, a boarding school, groomed them and began having sex with them at age 16, which state law considers old enough for consent.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: David Degner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matthew Rutledge, who was indicted on three counts of rape on Tuesday, taught at Miss Hall’s school in Pittsfield, Mass.

item.title: Somali Immigrants Have Effectively Been Denied a Fair Hearing, Lawsuit Says
item.description: Two Minnesota-based legal groups accused the Justice Department of unconstitutionally expediting Somalis’ immigration cases.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: The vast majority of the 80,000 Somalis who live in Minnesota are American citizens or legal permanent residents.

item.title: 2,000 U.S. Troops From 82nd Airborne Division to Be Sent to Middle East
item.description: The order gives President Trump more military options as he considers diplomacy with Iran. It is unclear where the soldiers will go in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Members of the 82nd Airborne Division in North Carolina in 2021. It is unclear where roughly 2,000 soldiers from the division will go in the Middle East, but the location would be within striking distance of Iran, officials said.

item.title: T.S.A. Tipped Off ICE Agents Before Arrests at San Francisco Airport
item.description: Transportation Security Administration officials told ICE that a mother and daughter under a detention order had planned to fly domestically, federal documents show.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Heather Knight
item.media:description: Angelina Lopez-Jimenez and her 9-year-old daughter were detained at the San Francisco International Airport and later deported to Guatemala.

item.title: Democrat Emily Gregory Wins Florida Special Election in Mar-a-Lago’s District
item.description: Emily Gregory’s victory in Palm Beach brought the Democratic surge to President Trump’s backyard, while a union leader leads in a race for a state senate seat vacated by Florida’s lieutenant governor.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: Josh Ritchie for The New York Times
item.media:description: Emily Gregory’s district includes President Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Fla.

item.title: Senate Republicans Again Block Bid to Halt Iran War Without Authorization
item.description: Senate Democrats failed for the third time to advance a resolution that would force the removal of troops from hostilities in Iran unless Congress approves offensive operations.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Christopher S. Murphy, Democrat of Connecticut, introduced Tuesday’s war powers resolution.

item.title: Driver Hops Curb Outside Iowa School, Injuring 9 Students
item.description: The injured students were taken to local hospitals after the crash, which the authorities said did not appear to be intentional.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

item.title: Flooding in Hawaii: What We Know
item.description: There were no deaths reported, but hundreds of people were rescued and thousands evacuated as a series of hard-hitting storms inundated the islands over the last two weeks.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:description: The flooding was largely caused by back-to-back storms that forecasters call a Kona low.

item.title: TSA Airport Delays: What It’s Like Waiting in Line for Hours
item.description: Scenes from extra long lines at two airports, and a surprising discovery at a third.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder, Shannon Sims and Mark Bonamo
item.media:description: Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport employees directing passengers in line at a Transportation Security Administration checkpoint on Monday.

item.title: Judge Orders Records Search After Trump Ties Cole Attack to Iran
item.description: The bombing of the ship by Al Qaeda killed 17 U.S. sailors in 2000. President Trump has said Iran was “probably involved.”
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: In all, 17 U.S. sailors were killed in the bombing of the Cole by Al Qaeda on Oct. 12, 2000. Family members of victims have sued Iran in federal court and won default judgments. Defense lawyers say they have seen no evidence of a link between Iran and the attack.

item.title: Kermit Gosnell, 85, Dies; Imprisoned Abortion Doctor Convicted of Murders
item.description: Philadelphia prosecutors in 2013 said he killed babies who had emerged alive during late-term abortions. The case became a rallying cry for anti-abortion activists.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel

item.title: Newsom Says He Regrets Remarks Comparing Israel to ‘Apartheid State’
item.description: The California governor suggested that he had meant to refer to Israel’s potential future direction, not its current policies. “I revere the state of Israel,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:description: Gov. Gavin Newsom of California has long been a supporter of Israel, but he has stepped up his criticism of its government more recently.

item.title: T.S.A. Union Leaders Blast Trump’s Deployment of ICE Agents in Airports
item.description: The local leaders of the union representing T.S.A. workers characterized the ICE officers in airports as unhelpful and a distraction.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers have been deployed to airports across the country amid a partial government shutdown.

item.title: N.C. Senator Phil Berger Officially Just Lost to Sam Page
item.description: Phil Berger had money, power and an endorsement from President Trump. But his critics had a long list of resentments and, on Tuesday, he lost by a mere 23 votes.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Berger is the country’s longest-serving active State Senate leader.

item.title: San Francisco Killed 8th-Grade Algebra. Now It’s Set to Come Back.
item.description: The San Francisco school board approved a plan to restore algebra as an option at all middle schools, more than a decade after it was removed over equity concerns.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Liz Hafalia/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The decision to stop offering algebra in middle school frustrated some families in the San Francisco Unified School District.

item.title: Trump Picks ‘Alpha Male’ Influencer to Be Tourism Envoy
item.description: Nick Adams, known for his crass humor and internet trolling, was previously nominated to be the ambassador to Malaysia. But his nomination was pulled earlier this year.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:description: President Trump picked Nick Adams for a newly created position: the “special presidential envoy for American tourism, exceptionalism and values.”

item.title: Florida Middle School Student Is Arrested After Stabbing Attack
item.description: A seventh-grade student was accused of stabbing two other students and a paraprofessional, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: The Walton County Sheriff’s Office responded to reports of a stabbing at Walton Middle School in DeFuniak Springs, Fla., on Tuesday.

item.title: High Oil and Gas Prices Could Outlast Trump’s War With Iran
item.description: While the president has promised rapid relief, Americans could feel the financial sting of the conflict for some time after it ends.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Even when the conflict ends, it may be weeks or months before American families and businesses see a true break in spiraling energy costs.

item.title: Marco Rubio Testifies in Corruption Trial Against Former U.S. Rep. David Rivera
item.description: To much ado, the secretary of state took the stand against David Rivera, a former congressman accused of secretly lobbying on behalf of the Venezuelan government.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams and Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio was called as a witness in a federal corruption trial in Miami, where he spent several hours testifying against an old friend.

item.title: Trump and Republican National Committee Lean Toward Dallas for Unusual 2026 GOP Midterm Convention
item.description: Parties normally hold conventions every four years to nominate presidential candidates, but Republicans hope to hold one this year in the face of midterm headwinds.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:description: President Trump has pushed for a major gathering before this year’s midterm elections to motivate his supporters, and Republicans are close to settling on Dallas as the location.

item.title: Minnesota Prosecutors Sue to Access Evidence in Shootings by Agents During Immigration Crackdown
item.description: The federal government has refused to provide even basic information about the three shootings that took place during an immigration crackdown in Minneapolis. Two were fatal.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A makeshift memorial for Alex Pretti, the second of two people shot and killed by federal agents in Minneapolis in January.

item.title: Russian Oil Shipment Puts Focus on Kremlin Spy Outpost in Cuba
item.description: Moscow may be challenging President Trump’s effort to choke Cuba’s economy. China also has suspected listening posts on the island.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Mr. Trump has spoken of using his economic leverage to mount a “friendly takeover” of Cuba and is pressing for the ouster of its president, Miguel Díaz-Canel.

item.title: California Governor Debate Canceled After Criticism Over Lack of Diversity
item.description: The debate would have featured six candidates, all white. The inclusion of a low-polling mayor drew scrutiny in particular.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: An earlier governor debate in San Francisco featured seven candidates, including the four entrants who were going to be excluded from a debate in Los Angeles on Tuesday.

item.title: Republicans Step Up Efforts to Reach DHS Funding Deal and End Shutdown
item.description: A day after President Trump said he did not want a compromise, Senate Republicans sent Democrats a plan that would break off ICE enforcement funding and reopen the rest of the department.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:description: Senate Republicans said they would seek to fund ICE enforcement operations through a separate bill that would not be subject to a Democratic filibuster.

item.title: Trump’s Threat to ‘Obliterate’ Iran’s Power Stations Could Constitute a War Crime, Rights Experts Say
item.description: Intentionally targeting the country’s energy infrastructure could constitute a war crime under international law.
item.dc:creator: Thomas Gibbons-Neff and John Ismay
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Tehran in 2024. President Trump threatened over the weekend to attack Iran’s power plants.

item.title: David A. Ross Sought Epstein’s Help to Build Art Museums. Now He’s Facing the Fallout.
item.description: He mastered the world of the “Epstein Class” to build great museums. Now he’s confronting the cost.
item.dc:creator: Eli Saslow and Erin Schaff
item.category: San Francisco Museum of Modern Art

item.title: Thomas Massie Thinks Being Hated by Trump Is ‘Worth It.’ Will Voters Agree?
item.description: The Republican congressman from Kentucky is a die-hard libertarian who has centered his campaign on his willingness to buck the president. It has bought him the most expensive primary in the country.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie is in the toughest fight of his political career.

item.title: In Oklahoma, Alan Armstrong Will Fill Markwayne Mullin’s Senate Seat
item.description: Gov. Kevin Stitt selected Mr. Armstrong, a fellow Republican and an energy executive, to play a caretaker role in the seat until the next election.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Alan Armstrong has led the board of directors at Williams Companies, a giant natural gas pipeline operator based in Tulsa, Okla., his hometown.

item.title: Saudi Leader Is Said to Push Trump to Continue Iran War in Recent Calls
item.description: Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman sees a “historic opportunity” to remake the region, according to people briefed by U.S. officials on the conversations.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump hosted Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman of Saudi Arabia at the White House last year.

item.title: 34 Former Military Members Were Put on Deportation Track in the Past Year
item.description: The Trump administration has ramped up enforcement against immigrant service members and their families in its wider crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jean Chung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sae Joon Park, who earned a Purple Heart for his service, left Hawaii for South Korea in June under the threat of deportation, in a high-profile example of the new policy.

item.title: Stephen Miller Asks Why Texas Pays to Teach Undocumented Children
item.description: Citing gridlock in Washington, President Trump’s top immigration adviser encouraged Texas lawmakers to lead on conservative priorities.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:description: Stephen Miller, President Trump’s immigration adviser, at the White House this month.

item.title: A Veteran Group Jumps In for a Democrat in Iowa Senate Race.
item.description: VoteVets is the first super PAC to intervene in the race for Josh Turek, a state legislator who was born with spina bifida after his father was exposed to Agent Orange in Vietnam.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:description: State Representative Josh Turek speaking to reporters after filing to appear on the Iowa ballot in the Democratic primary for U.S. Senate at the Iowa State Capitol earlier this month.

item.title: A Difficult Question for Teachers: What to Say About Cesar Chavez?
item.description: In classrooms across the country, educators are weighing whether to shift focus from Chavez to the broader labor movement he helped lead.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Vici MacDonald/San Francisco Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: United Farm Workers president Cesar Chavez, center, lead UFW picketers calling for a consumer boycott on Chiquita bananas in front of the Marina District Safeway supermarket in San Francisco in 1979.

item.title: Should You Need to Prove Citizenship to Vote? Ask Kansas.
item.description: A Kansas law required a passport, a birth certificate or other proof of citizenship to register, but it was struck down after a court found that around 31,000 eligible voters had been blocked.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Arin Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Kansas did that 10 years ago,” the secretary of state, Scott Schwab, said of a law that required people to provide proof of citizenship to register to vote. “It didn’t work out so well.”

item.title: Gregory Bovino’s Final Days: Harsh Words and Few Regrets
item.description: He was the face of the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown. But as he begins a retirement that was not entirely voluntary, the Border Patrol leader says he did not go far enough.
item.dc:creator: Katie J.M. Baker and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: Border Patrol official Gregory Bovino is retiring this week.

item.title: Supreme Court Seems Open to Trump Request to Block Asylum Seekers at Border
item.description: A policy of turning back many asylum seekers at the border was rescinded in 2021, but the Justice Department wants the flexibility to reinstate it as a tool for border control.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Migrants approved for asylum appointments with Customs and Border Protection lined up for permission to travel to the U.S. in Tapachula, Mexico, last year.
